figures.caption: Figure 4 LLM-optimization evaluation frameworks. One-shot evaluation: an optimization agent creates a formulation based on one question, and aims to match a ground truth. Empirical evaluation: an optimization agent is scored based on interactions with human decision-makers. Our approach: an optimization agent is scored based on interactions with decision agents based on verifiable and rigorous criteria.

Figure 5 The optimization agent’s architecture includes an LLM, prompt, and toolkit, allowing the agent to read/write (R/W) from/to the model, pass the model to the solver, derive its output, and summarize the solution for the decision-maker. This illustration corresponds to the T-P-P design.
Figure 8 Example of interaction between a decision agent and the C-P-P optimization agent.
Figure 9 Code used to modify the model implemented by the C-P-P agent following Figure 8
Table 2 Performance of the T-P-P optimization agent under three evaluation paradigms.
Figure 1 Visualization of approaches to optimization in practice.
Figure 6 Distribution of scores when evaluating the T-P-P agent under two evaluation paradigms.
Figure 11 Distribution of conversation length for each optimization agent across decision agents’ personalities.
Figure 2 Example of LLM-based autoformulation: a school principal describes an optimization problem in precise technical language and the tool returns a mathematical formulation of the problem.
Figure 10 Example of interaction between a decision agent and the T-P-P optimization agent.
Figure 3 Example of a conversational interaction: the principal is far less precise in their goals and requirements and the tool navigates the problem’s landscape to guide them to a good solution.
